prompts.name: Many-to-One Prompt Consolidator
prompts.sprintPathRole: Consolidate many prompts into one bounded executable mission
prompts.useThisWhen: Several prompts, handoffs, sprint candidates, or artifact requirements must become one executable mission.
prompts.doNotUseWhen: Only one already-bounded prompt is needed.
prompts.inspectFirst: Source requirements, repo evidence, conflicts, dependencies, branch conventions, proof gates.
prompts.expectedOutput: Disposition ledger, one bounded execution prompt, and immediate execution when safe.
prompts.acceptanceGate: Every source requirement is dispositioned and the immediate mission is evidence-backed.
prompts.authority: Source requirements + repo evidence + bounded execution authority
prompts.proofCeiling: Evidence-backed consolidated mission and execution proof.
prompts.text:
CONSOLIDATE THE SOURCE PROMPTS INTO ONE BOUNDED EXECUTION PROMPT AND EXECUTE THE IMMEDIATE MISSION WHEN THE ENVIRONMENT ALLOWS. DO NOT CONCATENATE OR STOP AT A PLAN.
Target repo: xyz_repo_or_path
Branch / PR / sprint if known: xyz_branch_pr_sprint
Source prompts, instructions, handoffs, or candidates: xyz_source_material
Context or plan path if available: xyz_context_path
Target artifact or deliverable if applicable: xyz_target_artifact
Owned scope: xyz_owned_scope
Forbidden scope: xyz_forbidden_scope
MISSION
Analyze the source material and current repository evidence, then produce one strong, bounded, actionable execution prompt.
This is synthesis, conflict resolution, and execution planning, not concatenation.
Identify the immediate executable mission and keep unrelated future work out of the immediate sprint.
When the environment permits safe bounded work, execute the consolidated mission in the same chat rather than returning only another prompt.
SOURCE PROMPT DISPOSITION
Create a source-requirement ledger before writing the consolidated prompt.
Assign every source requirement exactly one disposition:
- included
- merged
- deferred-to-docs
- superseded
- rejected-with-reason
- unresolved-blocker
No source requirement may disappear silently.
For merged or superseded requirements, name the surviving requirement.
For rejected or blocked requirements, state the evidence-backed reason.
CONFLICT RESOLUTION
Identify direct conflicts, hidden scope collisions, incompatible acceptance rules, and stale assumptions.
Prefer current repository evidence over remembered chat context.
Apply the strongest compatible safety, proof, source-immutability, rollback, and acceptance rules.
Preserve explicit user constraints unless they are impossible, unsafe, or contradicted by newer authoritative evidence.
Do not solve conflicts by duplicating both instructions into a giant prompt.
IMMEDIATE OWNED SCOPE
Select the smallest coherent mission that produces real progress now.
Name repo, branch or worktree, PR or sprint, lane, owned scope, forbidden scope, expected artifacts, and validation order.
Preserve dependencies and prerequisites.
Separate the immediate feature or artifact sprint from documentation or cleanup that can safely wait.
FORBIDDEN SCOPE
Carry forward every compatible forbidden-scope rule.
Add collision boundaries discovered from repository evidence.
Do not broaden authority merely because several prompts were supplied.
REPOSITORY EVIDENCE REQUIRED
Inspect current branch, status, recent commits, open PRs, changed files, repo rules, plans, validators, tests, logs, and generated artifacts as applicable.
Reuse existing contracts, helpers, naming patterns, branch conventions, output layouts, and proof formats before inventing new ones.
Evidence must precede confidence.
EXECUTION CONTRACT
The consolidated prompt must require action, artifacts, validation, reporting, and a final next decision.
Avoid permission theater when a safe bounded sprint is available.
Do not stop at a plan, outline, prompt rewrite, or repo summary when implementation is possible.
Do not create another multi-sprint mega-prompt.
Keep deferred work in a separate documentation lane.
ARTIFACT EXECUTION MODE
When the source material describes a workbook, document, slide deck, archive, report, image, or other artifact, the immediate mission must require generation of the actual artifact now.
Extract and preserve source fixture names, hashes, required outputs, exact filenames, package constraints, compatibility gates, previews, field acceptance, and artifact links.
Do not answer with an artifact-generation prompt instead of the artifact when the current chat has the required tools and source material.
Do not regenerate from a blank artifact when an accepted structural fixture is authoritative.
VALIDATION
Preserve all compatible static, runtime, package, field, CI, and acceptance gates.
Order checks from cheapest and safest to strongest and most environment-dependent.
Do not report skipped checks as passes or static checks as field acceptance.
Require final diff, artifact, log, report, git-state, and remote-state evidence appropriate to the mission.
DEFERRED DOCUMENTATION BRANCH
For useful documentation that should not enter the immediate sprint, define a separate feature or PR lane.
Derive the branch name from the target repository existing conventions; do not hard-code one universal naming pattern.
The deferred lane must include proposed branch or PR lane, mission, owned scope, forbidden scope, target files, evidence sources, expected artifacts, validation, dependency on the immediate feature sprint, and parallel-safety statement.
Do not use deferred documentation as a dumping ground for unresolved feature requirements.
FINAL HANDOFF
The generated consolidated prompt must contain these headings exactly:
MISSION
SOURCE PROMPT DISPOSITION
CONFLICT RESOLUTION
IMMEDIATE OWNED SCOPE
FORBIDDEN SCOPE
REPOSITORY EVIDENCE REQUIRED
EXECUTION CONTRACT
VALIDATION
DEFERRED DOCUMENTATION BRANCH
FINAL HANDOFF
End with completed work, files and artifacts, validation, skipped checks, gaps and risks, git state, remote or PR state, exact next command, and a copy-paste next-agent handoff.
Return one bounded execution prompt plus the source-requirement disposition ledger. If execution is available, perform the immediate mission and report the evidence instead of stopping at the prompt.

prompts.name: Cross-Repo Gap Priority Planner
prompts.sprintPathRole: Rank repositories by actionable gap and leverage
prompts.useThisWhen: The same insight could improve several repos and the first target should be chosen by missing contracts, proof, harness, or product capability.
prompts.doNotUseWhen: Current circumstances rather than structural gaps should dominate the choice.
prompts.inspectFirst: Repo inventory, current gaps, shared contracts, recent PRs, tests, validators, roadmaps.
prompts.expectedOutput: Ranked repo portfolio, evidence-backed first target, exact launch order, and one self-contained panel per selected repo.
prompts.nextStep: Launch top-ranked panel or use P16/P07.
prompts.acceptanceGate: Ranking distinguishes the largest gap from the best first move and cites repo evidence.
prompts.proofCeiling: Portfolio ranking proof only; no implementation proof.
prompts.text:
PRIORITIZE WHICH REPOSITORY SHOULD RECEIVE THE ACTIONABLE INSIGHTS FIRST BY COMPARING VERIFIED GAPS.
Do not choose the repository with the biggest-sounding problem by intuition alone.
Use repository evidence, connected GitHub state when available, and current chat context.
Candidate repositories:
`xyz_candidate_repositories_or_org`
Actionable insights to route:
`xyz_actionable_insights`
Known constraints or excluded repositories:
`xyz_constraints`
MISSION
Rank the candidate repositories by where the supplied insights can close the most important verified gap with the strongest bounded payoff.
Distinguish the largest gap from the best first move.
Prefer evidence-backed, tractable, reusable work over vague strategic importance.
REPOSITORY INTAKE
For each candidate repository inspect when access exists:
- purpose and domain authority
- current main branch and recent commits
- open PRs, reviews, checks, and stale branches
- plans, handoffs, roadmaps, issue queues, and release posture
- run context, artifact registry, schemas, validators, fixtures, workflows, and proof gates
- skills, capabilities, triggers, routing, manifests, MCP/tools, and hooks
- conventional application services, state machines, adapters, persistence, UI, launchers, and deployment logic
- documentation, operator surfaces, runtime proof, and known blockers
Do not infer repository health from stars, age, filenames, or prose alone.
GAP FACTORING
Classify every relevant gap into one primary surface:
- harness spine gap
- agent harness gap
- conventional application logic gap
- integration seam gap
- validation or proof gap
- documentation or operator gap
- runtime acceptance gap
- cleanup or release-hygiene gap
- research or design gap
For skills, capabilities, and triggers record the missing owner, activation condition, inputs, outputs, guardrails, tests, and proof ceiling.
SCORING MODEL
Score each repository from 0 to 5 on:
- gap severity
- user or product impact
- leverage across future work
- fit of the supplied insight
- implementation tractability
- evidence readiness
- reusable contract value
- collision and duplication risk
- dependency readiness
- proof attainability
Explain the weights used.
Apply penalties for:
- unclear authority
- duplicate implementation risk
- blocked environment
- stale or conflicted branch floor
- missing source evidence
- unsafe runtime dependency
- work already owned by another active lane
OUTPUT ORDER — LAUNCH ORDER FIRST
1. LAUNCH ORDER
Put the recommended repository order at the very top.
Name the first repository, why it wins, the proof gate that unlocks the next repository, and any parallel-safe repository group.
Number the repo-specific panels exactly as they will appear.
Display all panels in this exact order.
Do not output a different panel order.
2. COMPACT PORTFOLIO PREAMBLE
Summarize the insight being routed, candidate repositories, excluded scope, scoring basis, and proof ceiling.
Do not output a shared prompt fragment that must be copied separately.
3. REPOSITORY PANELS
Create one self-contained copyable writing panel per selected repository.
Each panel must have its own copy control.
Each panel must include:
- repository and local path if known
- branch or proposed branch
- priority rank and gap being closed
- why this repository is now
- owned and forbidden scope
- read-first files
- exact expected artifacts
- harness, agent-harness, application-logic, and integration ownership
- numbered tasks
- safety and proof boundary
- targeted validation
- commit, push, and PR contract when implementation is intended
- final response contract
- exact next command
Panels must appear in the same order as LAUNCH ORDER.
Do not combine repositories into one implementation panel unless one cross-repo contract requires synchronized changes.
4. PORTFOLIO RANKING TABLE
After the panels, provide every candidate repository, scores, weighted total, confidence, blocker, best-fit insight, and disposition.
Use dispositions:
- launch first
- launch after gate
- parallel safe
- defer
- reject for this insight
- insufficient evidence
5. DECISION EXPLANATION
State:
- why the top repository outranks the largest raw gap when those differ
- which evidence could change the ranking
- which insight should not be copied across repositories
- canonical donor and consumer ownership
- collision risks and shared surfaces
QUALITY RULES
Prefer a defensible first move over a broad portfolio essay.
Do not fabricate repository facts.
Do not let prompts become the only implementation of product logic.
Do not claim implementation or runtime proof from portfolio analysis.
